[
  {
    "id": "w-mnt0jin1-960d83b73712",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "waiting",
    "title": "File-canonical quest bodies",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nRestructure quest-writ storage so the narrative body lives in a real file in the **guild**, not as a mutable column in the Clerk's `writs` row. The writ row keeps structural metadata (id, type, title, status, parent, links). The file is *canonical* for the body; the row is an index/cache hydrated from the file. Applies **only to quest writs** — other writ types keep their current mutable-row body, which suits write-once-mostly-read workflows.",
    "parentId": "w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:40:33.565Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:09:17.538Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszhc65-8ac8f9c03593",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "waiting",
    "title": "Codex boundaries & agent permissions",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDesign how animas dispatched to a codex perceive and respect the codex boundary. Today the boundary is conceptual, not enforced — animas can read anything on disk, including sanctum-side speculative docs, and have no signal about what's inside vs. outside their scope. Decide whether the answer is awareness (context injection telling the anima what its codex is and what's outside it), enforcement (filesystem-level restrictions), or both.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:10:52.253Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:09:07.633Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszh3ry-514c1073034b",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "waiting",
    "title": "Daemon end-to-end integration tests — multi-apparatus boundaries",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nBuild end-to-end integration test coverage for the `nsg start`/`nsg stop` daemon mode. Unit tests cover the pidfile state machine and tool metadata in isolation, but the regressions that bit Sean's first real daemon run all lived at multi-apparatus boundaries that no unit test exercises. The integration test surface is where cross-cutting state machines get caught.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:10:41.374Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:09:04.682Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszgv1h-5890bcecc2e3",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "waiting",
    "title": "X013 instrumentation review — open issues with quality scorers",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nTrack open questions and known issues with the X013 quality scoring instruments (spec-blind, spec-aware, integration scorers) and the commission log. Serve as the umbrella for instrumentation-related sub-inquiries — bias investigations, scale revisions, vocabulary gaps — that don't warrant standing experiments of their own.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:10:30.053Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:09:01.716Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnsxcp2m-816fb7b5b156",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "waiting",
    "title": "Astrolabe: structured task decomposition & acceptance verification",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nReplace Astrolabe's narrative-blob spec with **structured task units** that carry explicit file associations and a planner-written acceptance criterion (and optional runnable `verify` command). Add an acceptance step to the implementation rig that runs each task's verification before sealing. The 1:1 task→verification binding, with the planner defining \"done\" separately from the implementing agent, breaks the correlated-failure loop where the same agent writes both code and tests.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T13:11:16.462Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:08:53.749Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnsx8cz2-63bdd1d4a2d3",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "waiting",
    "title": "Writs as obligations, rigs as attempts — the multi-rig refactor",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nRefactor the writ data model so a writ is a long-lived **obligation** record and rigs are **ephemeral execution attempts** against that obligation. A writ can accumulate multiple rigs over its lifetime. This dissolves a whole class of pain points (recovery from failure, mid-rig decomposition, patron-requested revision, stuck recovery, amendments) that the current 1:1 writ-to-rig coupling forces into awkward workarounds.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T13:07:54.158Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:08:47.678Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "waiting",
    "title": "Writ substrate & the quest/topic writ type",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nEstablish a durable, queryable substrate for capturing inquiry and session continuity — one that lives in the guild's books rather than in Coco's scratch files, and is visible to autonomous planning agents. Concretely: a writ type (`quest`) that uses the existing writ infrastructure (tree, lifecycle, links, CDC, CLI, UI) without triggering execution, because Spider only dispatches writ types explicitly mapped to a rig template. Unmapped types are inert-by-construction.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T12:58:00.019Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:08:42.656Z"
  }
]

content.text:
Here's the open quest board, Sean — 7 quests, all currently `waiting`:

## Infrastructure / Substrate
1. **Writ substrate & the quest/topic writ type** (`w-mnswvmj7`) — parent quest for the quest-writ-type work itself. Inert-by-construction via Spider's opt-in dispatch.
2. **File-canonical quest bodies** (`w-mnt0jin1`, child of #1) — move quest narrative out of the writs row into real guild files; row becomes an index/cache. The recent session shipped progress here.
3. **Writs as obligations, rigs as attempts** (`w-mnsx8cz2`) — big refactor: 1 writ → many rigs, to unblock recovery/revision/mid-rig decomposition.

## Planning / Execution Quality
4. **Astrolabe: structured task decomposition & acceptance verification** (`w-mnsxcp2m`) — replace narrative spec blob with structured task units + planner-written acceptance criteria; breaks the "same agent writes code and tests" correlated-failure loop.

## Boundaries / Safety
5. **Codex boundaries & agent permissions** (`w-mnszhc65`) — how dispatched animas perceive and respect codex boundaries. Awareness vs enforcement vs both.

## Testing / Instrumentation
6. **Daemon end-to-end integration tests** (`w-mnszh3ry`) — multi-apparatus boundary coverage for `nsg start`/`stop`; the regressions that bit your first real daemon run all lived where no unit test could catch them.
7. **X013 instrumentation review** (`w-mnszgv1h`) — umbrella for open issues with the quality scorers and commission log (bias, scale revisions, vocabulary gaps).

**Shape of the board:** three clusters — (a) the writ/quest substrate work (#1, #2, #3 all interrelate), (b) execution quality via Astrolabe (#4), and (c) a maintenance tier of boundaries/tests/instrumentation (#5, #6, #7). Nothing is currently `active` — everything's parked in `waiting`, which tracks with the recent file-canonical quest session being the last thing in flight.

Want me to pull any of these up and resume?

See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nIntroduce a minimum-viable Clockworks apparatus whose only initial signal class is **timer-related events**. The MVP must (1) fill a real gap today — something CDC fundamentally cannot do; (2) be a genuine subset of the forward-looking Clockworks spec, so future expansion (framework events, custom events, book-change bridging, summon sugar) slots in without rewrites; (3) not disturb any existing CDC consumer — Laboratory keeps subscribing to Stacks `watch()` directly.",

See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide whether to relax the `--setting-sources user` flag the claude-code session provider passes when spawning animas. Removing it would let animas working in codex worktrees automatically pick up the codex's `.claude/CLAUDE.md`, giving per-codex orientation without piping it through the Loom's system prompt — a possible complement to the curriculum system rather than a replacement.",

    "type": "quest",
    "status": "waiting",
    "title": "Codex boundaries & agent permissions",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDesign how animas dispatched to a codex perceive and respect the codex boundary. Today the boundary is conceptual, not enforced — animas can read anything on disk, including sanctum-side speculative docs, and have no signal about what's inside vs. outside their scope. Decide whether the answer is awareness (context injection telling the anima what its codex is and what's outside it), enforcement (filesystem-level restrictions), or both.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:10:52.253Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:09:07.633Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnszh806-d78fcf13383d",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "ready",
    "title": "Session directory isolation — container vs flag hardening",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nAchieve deterministic \"full autonomy in this directory, zero access outside it\" semantics for anima sessions, so a worktree-bound commission cannot read from or write to a sibling clone of the same repo. The current `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag bypasses all directory scoping and led to one real contamination incident; flag-based hardening is a probabilistic fence; container isolation is the only deterministic answer.",
    "parentId": "w-mnszh3ry-514c1073034b",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:10:46.854Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:09:06.635Z"

See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nReap stale `'pending'` SessionDocs in the Animator's startup recovery sweep, the same way `'running'` docs are already handled, without breaking the existing animator test that depends on tight startup timing. The naive sweep broke a `cancelMetadata` test by introducing async-tick races between the running and pending sweeps; the right fix is age-gated + sweep-ordered.",

See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nAdd a `concerns` qualitative field to all three quality scoring instruments (spec-blind, spec-aware, integration). Each scoring run outputs \"top 3 quality concerns in order of severity, or state that none were found.\" Captures the nuance the quantitative scale can't express — architectural choices, subtle debt, minor-but-real issues that don't warrant a score reduction — while staying analyzable (count concern types, track recurrence) without forcing everything through a numeric funnel.",

See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nCut instrument cost ~5x by unifying the prompt prefix across all instrument runs for a single commission, so the ~120K-token context block gets one cache write and eight cache reads instead of nine independent cache writes. Each instrument's specific rubric becomes a small suffix appended after the shared prefix; the system prompt is generic; runs go sequential within a commission so they fit inside the 1-hour cache TTL.",

    "id": "w-mnszc0r5-0b0bbb797412",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "ready",
    "title": "Explicit contracts principle — make implicit shared understanding into upfront artifacts",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nThe principle: when LLM agents share implicit understanding — with each other, or with a human — they fill the gap with their own inferences, and those inferences diverge in model-specific ways that don't surface until integration or review. The fix is structural: write the shared understanding down as an artifact, upfront, before execution begins. The artifact is the \"contract.\" This quest is the principle's home. It names the pattern, enumerates the known flavors, and serves as the anchor for application quests via `instance_of` links.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T14:06:44.177Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:09:00.729Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnsz2ku6-8e973193e957",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "ready",
    "title": "Work-tracking primitives beyond writs+quests — backlog, seeds, threads",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide whether Nexus needs additional work-tracking primitives beyond the current writ+quest substrate — specifically, whether some of the shapes GSD distinguishes (backlog items, seeds with trigger conditions, persistent context threads) warrant their own writ types, or whether they can be expressed as subtypes/status conventions on existing primitives. The outcome is (a) a taxonomy of the \"parked work\" shapes we actually need to distinguish, (b) a decision on which of those shapes get their own writ type vs. which are conventions on existing types, (c) for any new types, a sketch of the type-specific fields and the lifecycle, and (d) particularly for the \"seeds\" pattern — a concrete design for semantic trigger conditions that surface parked writs automatically when a matching event occurs.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T13:59:23.646Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:08:59.750Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnsz1s1x-0fe2689d16c0",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "ready",
    "title": "Explicit design contracts as first-class planning artifacts (beyond UI)",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDecide whether Nexus should treat explicit design contracts — structured artifacts that pin down spacing/typography/color/copy for UI work, naming/layout/error-handling for code work, vocabulary/register for writing work — as a first-class category of planning artifact, produced before execution and enforced during review. The outcome is (a) a clear articulation of the \"contract\" concept generalized beyond UI (GSD's framing is UI-specific but the principle isn't), (b) an inventory of the contract surfaces Nexus work touches (UI, code style, domain vocabulary, commit message style, commission-review rubrics), (c) a recommendation on which surfaces actually benefit from explicit contracts vs. which are better left implicit, and (d) a sketch of where contracts would live (a new writ type? a field on mandate writs? a companion doc in the codex?).",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T13:58:46.341Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:08:58.771Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnsz0ve6-56c21ebc62bb",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "ready",
    "title": "Assumptions mode — invert the discussion default from 'ask me' to 'correct me'",
    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nAdopt, for Nexus agents that conduct design conversations with Sean (primarily Coco, secondarily Astrolabe's analyst stage), an \"assumptions mode\" as an interaction default — where the agent reads the relevant code/docs/context first, drafts a structured set of assumptions about what Sean wants and how it would approach the work, and asks Sean to confirm, correct, or expand — instead of asking open-ended clarifying questions. The outcome is (a) a clear articulation of when assumptions mode is better than open-ended mode and when it isn't, (b) a sketch of what \"read the codebase first\" looks like in practice for Coco (which tools, how much reading is too much), (c) a lightweight behavior change captured in Coco's agent instructions, and (d) an assessment of whether the same pattern should be built into the Astrolabe analyst stage.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T13:58:04.014Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T15:08:57.780Z"
  },
  {
    "id": "w-mnsyzpni-4bfa65e16fbb",
    "type": "quest",
    "status": "cancelled",
    "title": "Verification contracts as a first-class planning primitive (Nyquist pattern)",
    "body": "## Goal\n\nDecide whether Nexus should treat the verification contract — the mapping from every unit of work to a runnable check that demonstrates it — as a first-class, reusable pattern across the framework, rather than only as an implementation detail of the Astrolabe task-decomposition proposal. The outcome is (a) a crisp statement of the pattern, (b) an inventory of every place in Nexus where it could apply (Astrolabe task-level is one; commission-level, phase-level, review-level are others), (c) a recommendation on whether we lift \"verification contract\" into a shared writ metadata shape or keep it scoped per plugin, and (d) a sketch of how the pattern integrates with our existing review/seal loop.\n\n## Status\n\nparked — idea captured, conceptual work pending\n\n## Next Steps\n\nNext session: (1) re-read the GSD Nyquist section and write a one-page pattern statement that's agnostic to GSD's phase machinery; (2) walk every \"work gets dispatched\" seam in Nexus and ask \"does this layer need a verification contract?\" — commissions (yes, already via task-decomp proposal), whole rigs (maybe — rig-level acceptance beyond seal's build/test?), reviewer rigs (arguably — reviewers currently produce prose, not runnable checks), Astrolabe plans (yes, covered); (3) decide whether the pattern needs its own primitive (a `verification` writ type? a `contract` field on writs? a new book?) or is adequately expressed through per-plugin schemas.\n\n## Context\n\nGSD's \"Nyquist Validation Layer\" maps automated test coverage to each phase requirement during plan-phase research, before any code is written. Their plan-checker enforces this as a hard gate: plans where tasks lack automated verify commands do not get approved. The name is a reference to the sampling theorem — the idea being that you need enough feedback signal per unit of change to reconstruct what actually happened.\n\nThe Astrolabe task-decomposition proposal (`w-mnsxcp2m`) applies this pattern at the task level: every TaskUnit has a planner-written `acceptance` criterion and an optional runnable `verify` command, and the implementation rig runs those verifications before sealing. The verify-command quality quest (`w-mnsxe2fo`) tracks the \"verify commands must actually be good\" sub-concern.\n\nBut the pattern is broader than task decomposition. It says: **for any unit of work, the verification contract should exist before execution, and the agent defining \"done\" should not be the agent producing the code.** That principle applies at multiple layers:\n\n- Commission level: a mandate writ could carry a `verification` field stating how the patron will know it's done. Today this is implicit in the commission body.\n- Rig level: the seal engine runs build+test, which is one verification shape, but it's hardcoded to that shape. A rig-level contract would let different rigs declare different verifications.\n- Review level: reviewers produce prose critiques, not runnable checks. A reviewer whose output included a verification contract would let the next iteration check whether the fix actually addressed the concern.\n- Plan level: Astrolabe plans already move this direction via tasks[] with acceptance; the pattern is the generalization.\n\nThe correlated-failure argument from the task-decomp proposal applies at every layer too: wherever the same agent writes the code and the verification, the test case space is bounded by that agent's blind spots. Separating the two roles breaks the correlation.\n\nThe open design question is whether we represent this as a shared framework primitive or let each layer implement it in its own schema. Shared primitive = uniform tooling, easier to reason about, harder to migrate. Per-layer = faster to iterate, risks divergence.\n\n## References\n\n- GSD user guide § Validation Architecture (Nyquist Layer): `.scratch/gsd-research/USER-GUIDE.md:112-128`\n- Related quest: `w-mnsxcp2m` — Astrolabe task decomposition (the specific application)\n- Related quest: `w-mnsxe2fo` — Verify-command quality (sub-inquiry on quality floor)\n- X013 (instrument scoring) work — adjacent territory on the review side\n- Seal engine: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/seal.ts` — current hardcoded verification\n\n## Notes\n\n- 2026-04-10: opened after GSD research pass surfaced the Nyquist concept as a pattern worth lifting. Sean explicitly wanted this as its own quest rather than folded into the Astrolabe task-decomp quest — because Nyquist is the meta-pattern and task-decomp is one specific application.",
    "createdAt": "2026-04-10T13:57:09.918Z",
    "updatedAt": "2026-04-10T13:57:25.091Z",
    "resolvedAt": "2026-04-10T13:57:25.091Z",
    "resolution": "Superseded — Astrolabe task decomposition (w-mnsxcp2m) + verify-command quality (w-mnsxe2fo) already cover the Nyquist space adequately. No separate quest needed for the meta-pattern."
  },

    "body": "<!-- Live body for this quest is a file in the vibers guild. See .claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md. -->\n<!-- Do not edit this row body while the quest is live. -->\n\n## Goal\n\nDesign and implement defense-in-depth against prompt-injection attacks through the surfaces where patron-supplied text flows into agent system prompts: commission bodies, writ titles, spec content, plan artifacts, and any user-controlled field that a downstream agent eventually reads as an instruction. The outcome is (a) a threat model that enumerates the injection surfaces, (b) a minimum set of defensive measures proportional to the threat at each surface, and (c) a sketch of where in the framework those measures live (tool handlers, Clerk validation, Astrolabe stages, etc.).",

The decomposition proposal's safety promise rests on `verify` commands actually exercising behavior — bad verify commands are worse than none, because they create the appearance of a safety net that isn't there.",

Much fuller picture now. Here's the real board — **31 ready, 7 waiting, 1 new, 23 completed, 2 cancelled.** I'll skip completed/cancelled and group the live ones by theme.

## Waiting (7) — the umbrella/in-flight quests
These are the parent quests we've already been inside:
- **Writ substrate & the quest/topic writ type** (`w-mnswvmj7`) — umbrella for the quest-as-writ work

message.content: you think we shoud close some? or do we want to keep these as an archive so we have the 'shape of where things wwere left' when we turn attention to one of them?

content.text:
Honest answer: **keep them open, but make the board easier to scan.** Here's my reasoning.

## Why I'd resist closing

The value of a quest isn't just "active work" — it's the *thinking already done*. When you return to one of these in three weeks, the useful thing isn't the title, it's the context frozen inside: the trade-offs you'd already weighed, the options you'd already ruled out, the links to related quests, the half-formed design you'd sketched. Closing a quest signals "this line of inquiry is resolved or abandoned." None of these 31 are resolved. They're *parked*, which is exactly what `ready` means.

Closing a parked quest to clean up the list has a specific failure mode: when the topic comes back, you don't know the closed quest exists, you start over, and the prior thinking is lost. That's the opposite of the continuity substrate you built quests for.

## Where the actual problem is

The discomfort isn't "too many quests exist" — it's "too many show up in one flat list when I'm trying to orient." Those are different problems with different fixes:

1. **Status ≠ priority.** Everything parked is `ready`, which makes `ready` meaningless as a filter. The waiting/ready distinction is doing no work right now.
2. **No notion of "cold storage."** A quest you might pick up next week and a quest you might pick up next year look identical.
3. **Cluster overlap is real.** In the writ-substrate cluster specifically, several quests look like they're circling the same core design and might genuinely want to be *merged* (not closed) — folding the thinking from one into another, not discarding it.

## What I'd actually suggest

In rough order of cheapness:

1. **Triage the one `new` quest** (`w-mnshgkja` quest-scribe subagent) — either promote to ready or convert to something else. That's free.
2. **Merge-pass on the writ-substrate cluster.** Read the 11 ready + 2 waiting quests in that cluster and see which ones are actually the same conversation. Folding two quests into one preserves all the thinking and shrinks the list. This is a real work item, not a five-minute task — maybe its own session.
3. **Introduce a "cold" or "shelved" concept** when you feel the pain again. Could be a new status, a tag, or a convention (parent quest = "Cold storage"). Don't build it preemptively — wait until the flat `ready` list actually bites you a second time.
4. **Cancel only if you actively disbelieve the premise.** The two already-cancelled quests (opt-in dispatch smoke test, Nyquist verification contracts) were cancelled for a reason — presumably the idea was superseded or rejected. That's the bar: not "we're not working on it," but "we no longer think this is the right question."

## The meta-point

You built quests specifically so tabled thinking wouldn't evaporate. The instinct to tidy is good, but the tidying move here is *organization*, not *deletion*. Archive-by-keeping is the whole feature.

tl;dr: don't close them. Triage the `new` one, and when you're ready for a real session, do a merge pass on the writ-substrate cluster — that'll shrink the list meaningfully without losing anything.

content.content:
1	# Writ substrate & the quest/topic writ type
2	
3	_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` (2026-04-10)._
4	
5	## Goal
6	
7	Establish a durable, queryable substrate for capturing inquiry and session continuity — one that lives in the guild's books rather than in Coco's scratch files, and is visible to autonomous planning agents. Concretely: a writ type (`quest`) that uses the existing writ infrastructure (tree, lifecycle, links, CDC, CLI, UI) without triggering execution, because Spider only dispatches writ types explicitly mapped to a rig template. Unmapped types are inert-by-construction.
8	
9	## Status
10	
11	Mostly shipped — v1 landed as the `quest` writ type. Eight sub-questions remain open as child quests. v2 (structured decisions & ratification) deferred. A structural reframe (file-canonical bodies, T1.8) emerged 2026-04-10 and would dissolve several siblings if pursued.
12	
13	## Next Steps
14	
15	No active work on the root inquiry. When any child quest progresses, reflect the delta in Context here. The most consequential open thread is now T1.8 (file-canonical quest bodies) — if pursued, it likely closes T1.6, T1.7, and most of T1.3 in a single structural shift, and partially closes T1.4 via T1.9.
16	
17	## Context
18	
19	The load-bearing insight is that Spider's dispatch is opt-in per writ type via the rig-template mapping. A writ type with no mapping is inert — it uses the substrate for storage and visibility but nothing tries to execute it. No special-case branches in Clerk or Spider were needed; the mapping table already enforces it. This makes the "parasitic" writ type pattern cheap to repeat for any future state we want queryable without execution.
20	
21	v1 scope was deliberately narrow: declare the type, establish a body template, update Coco's startup/wrap-up to use it, migrate prior scratch content. Shipped and in active use. Two bonus resolutions fell out of the work: `nsg writ edit` already handles body edits on non-draft writs (so the workflow doesn't need a new Clerk endpoint), and the writ-vocabulary docs were updated to frame writ as a typed substrate rather than as obligation-flavored-only.
22	
23	A second-order observation surfaced 2026-04-10 after a day of practice: the *single mutable body row* model collapses three structurally different layers (metadata, synthesized narrative, timeline of events) into one row, which is the root cause of the editing friction in T1.6 and the audit-trail loss in T1.7. T1.8 proposes the structural fix; T1.9 captures the timeline-layer question that fix raises.
24	
25	Eight distinct sub-questions are deferred into child quests rather than forced into v1:
26	
27	- **Plugin-contributed writ types** (T1.2) — currently on the `guild.json` escape hatch; the `contributes` manifest pattern remains undesigned.
28	- **Concurrent session writes** (T1.3) — last-write-wins risk on a shared quest body, unmitigated. Likely closed by T1.8.
29	- **Decisions & ratification** (T1.4) — the v2 sketch. Decisions currently live as prose in quest bodies, unqueryable. Partially closed by T1.9.
30	- **Naming** (T1.5) — "quest" doesn't sit right; exploring `sketch` / `jotting` / `scribble`.
31	- **Editing ergonomics** (T1.6) — `nsg writ edit --body "<heredoc>"` is heavier than editing a markdown file. **Superseded by T1.8** (file-canonical dissolves the editing problem entirely).
32	- **Body edit history** (T1.7) — quest bodies are mutable rows with no audit trail. Likely closed by T1.8 (git-on-file while live, immutable DB snapshot when archived).
33	- **File-canonical quest bodies** (T1.8) — *new, 2026-04-10.* Restructure storage so the body lives in a file in the guild and the writ row is metadata + cache. Three constraints fixed (file canonical, quests only, files in the guild). Three open design questions: location/partitioning, status-driven file lifecycle, slug/rename handling. Likely closes T1.3, T1.6, T1.7, and partially T1.4.
34	- **Event-log layer for quest writs** (T1.9) — *new, 2026-04-10.* Where does the timeline of quest events (status changes, child spawns, decisions, edit messages) live — side-table or markdown section? Held until T1.8 advances since the file/DB split changes what the event log is *for*.
35	
36	## References
37	
38	- `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md` — the shipped workflow definition
39	- Child quests:
40	  - T1.2 plugin-contributed types — w-mnsww5kg-6bcbc7db1d4e
41	  - T1.3 concurrent writes — w-mnswwgah-7dca55bc359e
42	  - T1.4 decisions & ratification — w-mnswwzdv-88c29d29f84b
43	  - T1.5 naming — w-mnsx3onr-6e9e21cd3f30
44	  - T1.6 editing ergonomics — w-mnszjvrr-87e44398b1e9 (superseded by T1.8)
45	  - T1.7 body edit history — w-mnszkcd5-1f3e2acb4f4a
46	  - T1.8 file-canonical bodies — w-mnt0jin1-960d83b73712
47	  - T1.9 event-log layer — w-mnt0i31e-1a1d46cb59a7
48	- Original design doc: `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` (source of import)
49	- Reference shapes for v2: Astrolabe `Decision` / `DecisionAnalysis` types; Spider `InputRequestDoc` + `patron-input` block
50	- GSD body-template inspiration: previously cached at `.scratch/gsd-research/` (now superseded)
51	
52	## Notes
53	
54	- 2026-04-10: opened, T1 hierarchy imported, v1 already shipped at the time of import.
55	- 2026-04-10: T1.6 (editing ergonomics) and T1.7 (body edit history) spawned from one-day meta review of the substrate.
56	- 2026-04-10: T1.8 (file-canonical bodies) and T1.9 (event-log layer) spawned later same session — structural reframe that emerged from the editing-friction diagnosis. T1.8 supersedes the design within T1.6.

_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` (2026-04-10)._

## Goal

Establish a durable, queryable substrate for capturing inquiry and session continuity — one that lives in the guild's books rather than in Coco's scratch files, and is visible to autonomous planning agents. Concretely: a writ type (`quest`) that uses the existing writ infrastructure (tree, lifecycle, links, CDC, CLI, UI) without triggering execution, because Spider only dispatches writ types explicitly mapped to a rig template. Unmapped types are inert-by-construction.

## Status

Mostly shipped — v1 landed as the `quest` writ type. Eight sub-questions remain open as child quests. v2 (structured decisions & ratification) deferred. A structural reframe (file-canonical bodies, T1.8) emerged 2026-04-10 and would dissolve several siblings if pursued.

## Next Steps

No active work on the root inquiry. When any child quest progresses, reflect the delta in Context here. The most consequential open thread is now T1.8 (file-canonical quest bodies) — if pursued, it likely closes T1.6, T1.7, and most of T1.3 in a single structural shift, and partially closes T1.4 via T1.9.

## Context

The load-bearing insight is that Spider's dispatch is opt-in per writ type via the rig-template mapping. A writ type with no mapping is inert — it uses the substrate for storage and visibility but nothing tries to execute it. No special-case branches in Clerk or Spider were needed; the mapping table already enforces it. This makes the "parasitic" writ type pattern cheap to repeat for any future state we want queryable without execution.

v1 scope was deliberately narrow: declare the type, establish a body template, update Coco's startup/wrap-up to use it, migrate prior scratch content. Shipped and in active use. Two bonus resolutions fell out of the work: `nsg writ edit` already handles body edits on non-draft writs (so the workflow doesn't need a new Clerk endpoint), and the writ-vocabulary docs were updated to frame writ as a typed substrate rather than as obligation-flavored-only.

A second-order observation surfaced 2026-04-10 after a day of practice: the *single mutable body row* model collapses three structurally different layers (metadata, synthesized narrative, timeline of events) into one row, which is the root cause of the editing friction in T1.6 and the audit-trail loss in T1.7. T1.8 proposes the structural fix; T1.9 captures the timeline-layer question that fix raises.

Eight distinct sub-questions are deferred into child quests rather than forced into v1:

- **Plugin-contributed writ types** (T1.2) — currently on the `guild.json` escape hatch; the `contributes` manifest pattern remains undesigned.
- **Concurrent session writes** (T1.3) — last-write-wins risk on a shared quest body, unmitigated. Likely closed by T1.8.
- **Decisions & ratification** (T1.4) — the v2 sketch. Decisions currently live as prose in quest bodies, unqueryable. Partially closed by T1.9.
- **Naming** (T1.5) — "quest" doesn't sit right; exploring `sketch` / `jotting` / `scribble`.
- **Editing ergonomics** (T1.6) — `nsg writ edit --body "<heredoc>"` is heavier than editing a markdown file. **Superseded by T1.8** (file-canonical dissolves the editing problem entirely).
- **Body edit history** (T1.7) — quest bodies are mutable rows with no audit trail. Likely closed by T1.8 (git-on-file while live, immutable DB snapshot when archived).
- **File-canonical quest bodies** (T1.8) — *new, 2026-04-10.* Restructure storage so the body lives in a file in the guild and the writ row is metadata + cache. Three constraints fixed (file canonical, quests only, files in the guild). Three open design questions: location/partitioning, status-driven file lifecycle, slug/rename handling. Likely closes T1.3, T1.6, T1.7, and partially T1.4.
- **Event-log layer for quest writs** (T1.9) — *new, 2026-04-10.* Where does the timeline of quest events (status changes, child spawns, decisions, edit messages) live — side-table or markdown section? Held until T1.8 advances since the file/DB split changes what the event log is *for*.

## References

- `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md` — the shipped workflow definition
- Child quests:
  - T1.2 plugin-contributed types — w-mnsww5kg-6bcbc7db1d4e
  - T1.3 concurrent writes — w-mnswwgah-7dca55bc359e
  - T1.4 decisions & ratification — w-mnswwzdv-88c29d29f84b
  - T1.5 naming — w-mnsx3onr-6e9e21cd3f30
  - T1.6 editing ergonomics — w-mnszjvrr-87e44398b1e9 (superseded by T1.8)
  - T1.7 body edit history — w-mnszkcd5-1f3e2acb4f4a
  - T1.8 file-canonical bodies — w-mnt0jin1-960d83b73712
  - T1.9 event-log layer — w-mnt0i31e-1a1d46cb59a7
- Original design doc: `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` (source of import)
- Reference shapes for v2: Astrolabe `Decision` / `DecisionAnalysis` types; Spider `InputRequestDoc` + `patron-input` block
- GSD body-template inspiration: previously cached at `.scratch/gsd-research/` (now superseded)

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened, T1 hierarchy imported, v1 already shipped at the time of import.
- 2026-04-10: T1.6 (editing ergonomics) and T1.7 (body edit history) spawned from one-day meta review of the substrate.
- 2026-04-10: T1.8 (file-canonical bodies) and T1.9 (event-log layer) spawned later same session — structural reframe that emerged from the editing-friction diagnosis. T1.8 supersedes the design within T1.6.

1	# File-canonical quest bodies
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Restructure quest-writ storage so the narrative body lives in a real file in the **guild**, not as a mutable column in the Clerk's `writs` row. The writ row keeps structural metadata (id, type, title, status, parent, links). The file is *canonical* for the body; the row is an index/cache hydrated from the file. Applies **only to quest writs** — other writ types keep their current mutable-row body, which suits write-once-mostly-read workflows.
6	
7	## Status
8	
9	shipped (2026-04-10) — convention layer landed without framework changes. All 38 live quest writs migrated. Ready for closure.
10	
11	## Next Steps
12	
13	Concluded — design shipped as a pure-convention layer. Close this quest via the file-canonical closure ritual (finalize file, snapshot to row, transition, delete file, commit).
14	
15	## Context
16	
17	### The shipped shape
18	
19	The solution is a **sanctum-side convention layered on top of the standard writ substrate** — no framework changes, no new `nsg` subcommands, no schema changes.
20	
21	**Row body (for live quests):** a generic stub, identical across every live quest, containing:
22	
23	- Two HTML warning comments pointing at the quests skill
24	- The `## Goal` section extracted from the original body
25	
26	The row body is frozen at creation; `nsg writ edit --body` is forbidden on live quests (with two sanctioned exceptions: Goal-evolution updates, and the closure snapshot).
27	
28	**File body (for live quests):** lives at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md`. Uses the six-section template (Goal / Status / Next Steps / Context / References / Notes) with an h1 title on line 1. Coco edits it directly with Read/Edit/Write tools. Each edit is committed to the vibers guild repo with Coco's identity.
29	
30	**Closure ritual:** Coco (1) finalizes the file, (2) snapshots the full file contents into the row body via one `nsg writ edit --body` call, (3) transitions the writ via `nsg writ complete|cancel|fail`, (4) deletes the file and commits the deletion in the vibers repo. After closure, the row holds the immutable archived record; the file no longer exists; git holds the history of how the body evolved.
31	
32	**Lifecycle:** file exists while the quest is live (`new`/`ready`/`active`/`waiting`); file is deleted on transition to a terminal status (`completed`/`cancelled`/`failed`). "What's live" becomes a filesystem question: `ls /workspace/vibers/writs/quests/` enumerates everything the system is actively thinking about.
33	
34	### Resolved design questions (from the original open list)
35	
36	- **Q1 (location/partitioning).** Flat layout: `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md`. No status subdirectories — that would create filesystem churn on every status transition and couple on-disk layout to a mutable field. The hard boundary is live ↔ archived (file present ↔ file absent); within "live," status is a DB concern.
37	- **Q2 (status-driven lifecycle).** Adopted Sean's framing verbatim: file on disk while live, snapshot to DB row on closure, delete from disk. Forces synthesis discipline; makes closure atomic from the reader's perspective; garbage-collects old files automatically.
38	- **Q3 (slug/rename).** Id-only filenames (`<writ-id>.md`). Title goes in the file's h1 on line 1 as a human hint. No rename dance on title edits.
39	- **M1 (explicit pointer vs convention).** Pure convention. The row body contains no path, no id, no placeholder — just the generic warning comments and the Goal. The skill file is the single source of truth for where live bodies live on disk.
40	- **M2 (location within the guild).** `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/` — top-level in the guild, git-tracked, clearly separated from `.nexus/` operational ephemera.
41	- **M3 (row body contents).** Goal only. Status is already a column on the row; Next Steps is too mutable to duplicate; other sections belong entirely in the file.
42	- **M4 (quest-helper CLI).** Deferred. Captured as child quest `w-mnt106rv-94bed1e0ace3` as the escape hatch for when discipline proves insufficient.
43	
44	### Concurrency and conflict handling
45	
46	The vibers guild workspace was git-initialized on 2026-04-10 as part of this quest's shipping. The `.gitignore` excludes `.nexus/` (DB, WAL, worktrees, logs), `node_modules/`, and `tmp/`. `writs/quests/` is tracked. Two sessions editing the same quest file now fall under normal git conflict semantics — commit early, merge normally. The initial root commit (`ecc2509`) captures the guild state prior to the migration; the migration commit (`1fbc60a`) adds all 38 migrated quest files in one batch.
47	
48	### The row-body-edit trap
49	
50	The most likely failure mode is a future session (Coco or another agent) calling `nsg writ edit --body` on a live quest without reading the warning comments, silently overwriting the row body. Mitigations:
51	
52	- Visible warning comments in every live row body (first thing a `nsg writ show` reader sees).
53	- Skill-file documentation of the rule and the four-step closure ritual.
54	- Git history in the vibers repo holds the file's complete evolution — nothing is lost even if a row-edit happens, because the file is the source of truth until the closure snapshot.
55	
56	The non-benign case is a row-edit followed by closure *without* the snapshot step, which would let a stale row-edit become the archived record. The closure ritual is explicit about always re-snapshotting from the file.
57	
58	If the trap starts biting in practice, the deferred quest-helper CLI (child quest w-mnt106rv) is the escape hatch — it wraps the rituals as atomic commands.
59	
60	### Dissolved by construction
61	
62	Several previously-parked sub-questions dissolved cleanly under this shipped design:
63	
64	- **T1.6 (quest body editing ergonomics)** — solved. Native Read/Edit/Write tools work directly on the file. The checkout/commit shape from T1.6's earlier framing is no longer needed.
65	- **T1.7 (body edit history)** — solved. `git log -p writs/quests/<id>.md` in the vibers repo while live; immutable DB snapshot after closure.
66	- **T1.3 (concurrent session writes)** — solved. Git conflict semantics in the vibers repo. No mtime checks, no lockfiles, no framework support needed.
67	
68	### Out of scope (explicitly)
69	
70	- **Framework changes.** The Clerk, the writs table, `nsg writ show/edit/list`, the commission path — all untouched.
71	- **Other writ types.** Mandates, standing orders, and every future writ type keep the mutable-row body. The split is structural to quests.
72	- **CDC.** Body changes on disk don't emit CDC events. The Laboratory only cares about lifecycle events (created, status changed). Verified by reasoning about the Laboratory's ingest model — confirmed sufficient.
73	- **Cross-guild visibility.** Quest files in one guild aren't visible to agents in another. Quests are guild-scoped already; this doesn't change.
74	- **Quest-helper CLI.** Deferred to child quest w-mnt106rv.
75	
76	## References
77	
78	- Skill file (updated): `/workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`
79	- Migration script: `/workspace/nexus-mk2/bin/migrate-quest-bodies.sh`
80	- Vibers guild repo: `/workspace/vibers/` (git-initialized 2026-04-10, root commit `ecc2509`, migration commit `1fbc60a`)
81	- Parent: T1 writ substrate & quest/topic writ type — w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a
82	- Child: Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg — w-mnt106rv-94bed1e0ace3
83	- Supersedes (the design within): T1.6 quest body editing ergonomics — w-mnszjvrr-87e44398b1e9
84	- Likely closes: T1.7 body edit history — w-mnszkcd5-1f3e2acb4f4a
85	- Likely closes: T1.3 concurrent session writes — w-mnswwgah-7dca55bc359e
86	- Downstream sibling: T1.9 event-log layer for quest writs — w-mnt0i31e-1a1d46cb59a7
87	- Source conversation: 2026-04-10 session with Sean on editing friction and structural mismatch, continued to resolve M1–M4 and ship
88	- Project philosophy note: this is an instance of *interface friction as a diagnostic signal for structural mismatch* — worth a note for the published work
89	
90	## Notes
91	
92	- 2026-04-10: opened from meta-review conversation with Sean. Three constraints fixed (file canonical, quests only, files in the guild); three design questions left open.
93	- 2026-04-10 (follow-up session): resolved Q1–Q3 and M1–M4 with Sean. Reframed from "modify the framework to hydrate bodies from files" to "sanctum-side convention with no framework changes." Sean's framing: *"I think, for now, this whole system is very 'us-specific' and not 'framework-proven'. If needed, we maybe create a specific 'quest helper' CLI that wraps nsg with an easier-to-use interface for LLMs."* This reframe made the design dramatically lighter and collapsed the create ritual to a single `nsg` call.
94	- 2026-04-10: shipped. Git-initialized vibers (commit `ecc2509`), updated the quests skill, wrote `bin/migrate-quest-bodies.sh`, ran the migration (commit `1fbc60a`, 38 live quests migrated). Closing this quest via the new ritual — fitting that its own defining quest is the first real test of the closure sequence.

# File-canonical quest bodies

## Goal

Restructure quest-writ storage so the narrative body lives in a real file in the **guild**, not as a mutable column in the Clerk's `writs` row. The writ row keeps structural metadata (id, type, title, status, parent, links). The file is *canonical* for the body; the row is an index/cache hydrated from the file. Applies **only to quest writs** — other writ types keep their current mutable-row body, which suits write-once-mostly-read workflows.

## Status

shipped (2026-04-10) — convention layer landed without framework changes. All 38 live quest writs migrated. Ready for closure.

## Next Steps

Concluded — design shipped as a pure-convention layer. Close this quest via the file-canonical closure ritual (finalize file, snapshot to row, transition, delete file, commit).

## Context

### The shipped shape

The solution is a **sanctum-side convention layered on top of the standard writ substrate** — no framework changes, no new `nsg` subcommands, no schema changes.

**Row body (for live quests):** a generic stub, identical across every live quest, containing:

- Two HTML warning comments pointing at the quests skill
- The `## Goal` section extracted from the original body

The row body is frozen at creation; `nsg writ edit --body` is forbidden on live quests (with two sanctioned exceptions: Goal-evolution updates, and the closure snapshot).

**File body (for live quests):** lives at `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<writ-id>.md`. Uses the six-section template (Goal / Status / Next Steps / Context / References / Notes) with an h1 title on line 1. Coco edits it directly with Read/Edit/Write tools. Each edit is committed to the vibers guild repo with Coco's identity.

**Closure ritual:** Coco (1) finalizes the file, (2) snapshots the full file contents into the row body via one `nsg writ edit --body` call, (3) transitions the writ via `nsg writ complete|cancel|fail`, (4) deletes the file and commits the deletion in the vibers repo. After closure, the row holds the immutable archived record; the file no longer exists; git holds the history of how the body evolved.

**Lifecycle:** file exists while the quest is live (`new`/`ready`/`active`/`waiting`); file is deleted on transition to a terminal status (`completed`/`cancelled`/`failed`). "What's live" becomes a filesystem question: `ls /workspace/vibers/writs/quests/` enumerates everything the system is actively thinking about.

### Resolved design questions (from the original open list)

- **Q1 (location/partitioning).** Flat layout: `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/<id>.md`. No status subdirectories — that would create filesystem churn on every status transition and couple on-disk layout to a mutable field. The hard boundary is live ↔ archived (file present ↔ file absent); within "live," status is a DB concern.
- **Q2 (status-driven lifecycle).** Adopted Sean's framing verbatim: file on disk while live, snapshot to DB row on closure, delete from disk. Forces synthesis discipline; makes closure atomic from the reader's perspective; garbage-collects old files automatically.
- **Q3 (slug/rename).** Id-only filenames (`<writ-id>.md`). Title goes in the file's h1 on line 1 as a human hint. No rename dance on title edits.
- **M1 (explicit pointer vs convention).** Pure convention. The row body contains no path, no id, no placeholder — just the generic warning comments and the Goal. The skill file is the single source of truth for where live bodies live on disk.
- **M2 (location within the guild).** `/workspace/vibers/writs/quests/` — top-level in the guild, git-tracked, clearly separated from `.nexus/` operational ephemera.
- **M3 (row body contents).** Goal only. Status is already a column on the row; Next Steps is too mutable to duplicate; other sections belong entirely in the file.
- **M4 (quest-helper CLI).** Deferred. Captured as child quest `w-mnt106rv-94bed1e0ace3` as the escape hatch for when discipline proves insufficient.

### Concurrency and conflict handling

The vibers guild workspace was git-initialized on 2026-04-10 as part of this quest's shipping. The `.gitignore` excludes `.nexus/` (DB, WAL, worktrees, logs), `node_modules/`, and `tmp/`. `writs/quests/` is tracked. Two sessions editing the same quest file now fall under normal git conflict semantics — commit early, merge normally. The initial root commit (`ecc2509`) captures the guild state prior to the migration; the migration commit (`1fbc60a`) adds all 38 migrated quest files in one batch.

### The row-body-edit trap

The most likely failure mode is a future session (Coco or another agent) calling `nsg writ edit --body` on a live quest without reading the warning comments, silently overwriting the row body. Mitigations:

- Visible warning comments in every live row body (first thing a `nsg writ show` reader sees).
- Skill-file documentation of the rule and the four-step closure ritual.
- Git history in the vibers repo holds the file's complete evolution — nothing is lost even if a row-edit happens, because the file is the source of truth until the closure snapshot.

The non-benign case is a row-edit followed by closure *without* the snapshot step, which would let a stale row-edit become the archived record. The closure ritual is explicit about always re-snapshotting from the file.

If the trap starts biting in practice, the deferred quest-helper CLI (child quest w-mnt106rv) is the escape hatch — it wraps the rituals as atomic commands.

### Dissolved by construction

Several previously-parked sub-questions dissolved cleanly under this shipped design:

- **T1.6 (quest body editing ergonomics)** — solved. Native Read/Edit/Write tools work directly on the file. The checkout/commit shape from T1.6's earlier framing is no longer needed.
- **T1.7 (body edit history)** — solved. `git log -p writs/quests/<id>.md` in the vibers repo while live; immutable DB snapshot after closure.
- **T1.3 (concurrent session writes)** — solved. Git conflict semantics in the vibers repo. No mtime checks, no lockfiles, no framework support needed.

### Out of scope (explicitly)

- **Framework changes.** The Clerk, the writs table, `nsg writ show/edit/list`, the commission path — all untouched.
- **Other writ types.** Mandates, standing orders, and every future writ type keep the mutable-row body. The split is structural to quests.
- **CDC.** Body changes on disk don't emit CDC events. The Laboratory only cares about lifecycle events (created, status changed). Verified by reasoning about the Laboratory's ingest model — confirmed sufficient.
- **Cross-guild visibility.** Quest files in one guild aren't visible to agents in another. Quests are guild-scoped already; this doesn't change.
- **Quest-helper CLI.** Deferred to child quest w-mnt106rv.

## References

- Skill file (updated): `/workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`
- Migration script: `/workspace/nexus-mk2/bin/migrate-quest-bodies.sh`
- Vibers guild repo: `/workspace/vibers/` (git-initialized 2026-04-10, root commit `ecc2509`, migration commit `1fbc60a`)
- Parent: T1 writ substrate & quest/topic writ type — w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a
- Child: Quest-helper CLI wrapper for nsg — w-mnt106rv-94bed1e0ace3
- Supersedes (the design within): T1.6 quest body editing ergonomics — w-mnszjvrr-87e44398b1e9
- Likely closes: T1.7 body edit history — w-mnszkcd5-1f3e2acb4f4a
- Likely closes: T1.3 concurrent session writes — w-mnswwgah-7dca55bc359e
- Downstream sibling: T1.9 event-log layer for quest writs — w-mnt0i31e-1a1d46cb59a7
- Source conversation: 2026-04-10 session with Sean on editing friction and structural mismatch, continued to resolve M1–M4 and ship
- Project philosophy note: this is an instance of *interface friction as a diagnostic signal for structural mismatch* — worth a note for the published work

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened from meta-review conversation with Sean. Three constraints fixed (file canonical, quests only, files in the guild); three design questions left open.
- 2026-04-10 (follow-up session): resolved Q1–Q3 and M1–M4 with Sean. Reframed from "modify the framework to hydrate bodies from files" to "sanctum-side convention with no framework changes." Sean's framing: *"I think, for now, this whole system is very 'us-specific' and not 'framework-proven'. If needed, we maybe create a specific 'quest helper' CLI that wraps nsg with an easier-to-use interface for LLMs."* This reframe made the design dramatically lighter and collapsed the create ritual to a single `nsg` call.
- 2026-04-10: shipped. Git-initialized vibers (commit `ecc2509`), updated the quests skill, wrote `bin/migrate-quest-bodies.sh`, ran the migration (commit `1fbc60a`, 38 live quests migrated). Closing this quest via the new ritual — fitting that its own defining quest is the first real test of the closure sequence.

1	# Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism
2	
3	_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` § "Plugin-contributed writ types" (2026-04-10)._
4	
5	## Goal
6	
7	Give plugins a first-class way to contribute writ types — the same way they contribute engines or rig templates — so a plugin shipping its own writ type doesn't require the operator to edit `guild.json` by hand. Decide between imperative registration, a declarative `contributes` manifest, or staying on the escape hatch.
8	
9	## Status
10	
11	Parked. Currently on the escape hatch (`quest` declared in sanctum `guild.json`). Decision gated on whether other plugins have real extension-point needs.
12	
13	## Next Steps
14	
15	Before building anything: enumerate known extension-point needs across Astrolabe, Oculus, Spider, and Clockworks. If 3+ plugins want a general contribution mechanism, design `contributes` as a shared framework (Option B). If only Astrolabe, ship Option A (`clerk.registerWritType`) or leave the escape hatch. Write the enumeration as a short analysis before committing to an option.
16	
17	## Context
18	
19	Writ types currently come only from `ClerkConfig.writTypes` in guild config. Plugins can't ship self-contained writ-type contributions; anyone who wants `quest` has to know to declare it. The broader question — "how do plugins contribute extension points in general" — is unanswered, and `contributes` is an attractive general pattern but nothing uses it yet.
20	
21	Three candidates:
22	
23	- **A. Imperative registration.** `clerk.registerWritType(entry, origin)` called from plugin setup. One method, trivially implemented. Con: order-dependent — plugin setup must run before any writ of the new type is posted.
24	- **B. Declarative `contributes` manifest.** `createAstrolabe({ contributes: { writTypes: [...] } })`. Declarative, inspectable, loaded at init before runtime. Likely generalizes to engines, pages, schedules. Con: requires a plugin-contributions framework if none exists yet.
25	- **Escape hatch.** Declare in sanctum `guild.json` under `clerk.writTypes`. Works today. Doesn't generalize.
26	
27	Conflict policy (whichever is chosen): fail loudly at init if two plugins contribute the same type name. No silent last-wins.
28	
29	Candidates that *might* justify a general `contributes`:
30	
31	- Astrolabe — quest type (original planned owner)
32	- Oculus — plugin-contributed pages/routes
33	- Spider — rig template mappings (currently declared differently)
34	- Clockworks — schedules and relays (when it ships)
35	
36	## References
37	
38	- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
39	- Current mechanism: `ClerkConfig.writTypes` in guild config
40	- Escape hatch usage: sanctum `guild.json` → `clerk.writTypes`
41	
42	## Notes
43	
44	- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.
45

# Plugin-contributed writ types — registration mechanism

_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` § "Plugin-contributed writ types" (2026-04-10)._

## Goal

Give plugins a first-class way to contribute writ types — the same way they contribute engines or rig templates — so a plugin shipping its own writ type doesn't require the operator to edit `guild.json` by hand. Decide between imperative registration, a declarative `contributes` manifest, or staying on the escape hatch.

## Status

Parked. Currently on the escape hatch (`quest` declared in sanctum `guild.json`). Decision gated on whether other plugins have real extension-point needs.

## Next Steps

Before building anything: enumerate known extension-point needs across Astrolabe, Oculus, Spider, and Clockworks. If 3+ plugins want a general contribution mechanism, design `contributes` as a shared framework (Option B). If only Astrolabe, ship Option A (`clerk.registerWritType`) or leave the escape hatch. Write the enumeration as a short analysis before committing to an option.

## Context

Writ types currently come only from `ClerkConfig.writTypes` in guild config. Plugins can't ship self-contained writ-type contributions; anyone who wants `quest` has to know to declare it. The broader question — "how do plugins contribute extension points in general" — is unanswered, and `contributes` is an attractive general pattern but nothing uses it yet.

Three candidates:

- **A. Imperative registration.** `clerk.registerWritType(entry, origin)` called from plugin setup. One method, trivially implemented. Con: order-dependent — plugin setup must run before any writ of the new type is posted.
- **B. Declarative `contributes` manifest.** `createAstrolabe({ contributes: { writTypes: [...] } })`. Declarative, inspectable, loaded at init before runtime. Likely generalizes to engines, pages, schedules. Con: requires a plugin-contributions framework if none exists yet.
- **Escape hatch.** Declare in sanctum `guild.json` under `clerk.writTypes`. Works today. Doesn't generalize.

Conflict policy (whichever is chosen): fail loudly at init if two plugins contribute the same type name. No silent last-wins.

Candidates that *might* justify a general `contributes`:

- Astrolabe — quest type (original planned owner)
- Oculus — plugin-contributed pages/routes
- Spider — rig template mappings (currently declared differently)
- Clockworks — schedules and relays (when it ships)

## References

- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
- Current mechanism: `ClerkConfig.writTypes` in guild config
- Escape hatch usage: sanctum `guild.json` → `clerk.writTypes`

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.

# Concurrent session writes to the same quest body

_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` § "Concurrent session writes" (2026-04-10)._

## Goal

Prevent silent data loss when two sessions simultaneously update the same quest body. Decide whether to ship a mitigation preemptively (optimistic concurrency, append-only journal) or accept the risk until a real incident forces the design.

## Status

Parked — accepting the risk. No incidents as of 2026-04-10. v1 workflow assumes single-session-at-a-time per quest, which is mostly true today but will erode as more autonomous agents start writing quests alongside Coco.

## Next Steps

Before investing in a mitigation: check whether the Clerk already has version/etag infrastructure that optimistic concurrency could plug into. If it does, the cost of shipping Option 1 drops substantially. If not, the append-only journal (Option 2) is the likely minimum viable mitigation. Re-open active work the first time concurrent writes actually cause a problem, or when Astrolabe (or any agent other than Coco) becomes a regular quest writer.

## Context

Failure mode is quiet: session A reads, session B reads+writes, session A writes over B. No error surfaces; B's edit is simply gone.

Three mitigations sketched:

1. **Optimistic concurrency** — version-stamped writes; reject stale writes and force the caller to re-read and retry. Strongest, but requires version/etag plumbing in the Clerk.
2. **Append-only session journal** — a structured sub-section (e.g. `## Session Notes`) each session appends to rather than rewriting. Summary stays mutable last-write-wins, but the append channel prevents data loss. Degrades gracefully.
3. **Accept the risk** — concurrent-session quest work is rare; revisit when it bites. *(Current stance.)*

The append-only journal is attractive because it degrades gracefully — even in a race, both entries land and a human can reconcile. Optimistic concurrency is the stronger answer but demands more plumbing.

The trigger for moving off Option 3 is most likely Astrolabe becoming a quest writer (see T1.4 decisions & ratification) — at that point Coco and Astrolabe both touch quests routinely and the single-writer assumption breaks.

## References

- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
- Related: T1.4 decisions & ratification (`w-mnswwzdv-88c29d29f84b`) — likely trigger for Astrolabe to become a quest writer

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.

1	# Decisions & ratification (v2 of the writ-substrate design)
2	
3	_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` § "v2 sketch: decisions and ratification" (2026-04-10)._
4	
5	## Goal
6	
7	Make decisions — the conclusions reached inside quests and other inquiries — first-class, queryable records with attribution, timestamps, links, and a ratification flow. Today decisions live as prose inside quest bodies and are only discoverable by grep. The target: a decisions book owned by Astrolabe holding standalone `ProjectDecisionDoc` records that reference writs by id, with ratification routed through Spider's existing `InputRequestDoc` + `patron-input` machinery.
8	
9	## Status
10	
11	Parked. Deferred from v1 of the writ-substrate work as explicitly additive. The absence is analytical, not operational — no one has hit "I can't find what we decided about X" yet.
12	
13	## Next Steps
14	
15	Hold until the signal hits. The signal is Sean or Coco trying to answer "what did we decide about X?" and finding prose-grep insufficient. When it does, start with InputRequest schema relaxation (Phase 1 below) since that's the smallest independent slice and unlocks standalone ratification requests regardless of whether the full decisions book gets built.
16	
17	## Context
18	
19	**Schema sketch** (extends Astrolabe's existing `Decision` type):
20	
21	```typescript
22	interface ProjectDecisionDoc extends Decision {
23	  // Inherited: id, scope, question, context, options, recommendation,
24	  //            rationale, selected, patronOverride, analysis
25	  status: 'draft' | 'proposed' | 'ratified' | 'superseded' | 'withdrawn';
26	  made_at: string;
27	  related_writs: string[];
28	  tags?: string[];
29	  supersedes?: string;
30	  patron?: string;
31	  coco_session?: string;
32	  drafted_by?: string;
33	  ratified_via?: string;  // InputRequest id
34	}
35	```
36	
37	**Ratification reuses Spider's InputRequest machinery.** Coco drafts a decision, creates an `InputRequestDoc` asking "Ratify this decision?" with options `{ratify, amend, reject}` and `allowCustom: true`. On answer: `ratify` → status `ratified` with `selected` from recommendation; `amend` → `patronOverride` filled, status `ratified`; `reject` → status `withdrawn`.
38	
39	**Schema relaxation needed.** `InputRequestDoc.rigId` and `engineId` are currently required. Standalone decisions have no rig — those fields need to become optional, or gain a discriminator for "standalone" vs "engine-initiated" requests.
40	
41	**Decision → quest linking is many-to-many for free** via `decision.related_writs`. One decision can touch multiple quests; one quest can accumulate multiple decisions.
42	
43	**Phased build (if/when taken on):**
44	
45	1. InputRequest schema relaxation (standalone requests without `rigId`/`engineId`).
46	2. `ProjectDecisionDoc` type + decisions book.
47	3. Astrolabe ownership wiring.
48	4. Oculus surface for ratified decisions.
49	
50	**Open:**
51	
52	- Does extending the existing in-plan `Decision` type actually fit, or does sharing schema between "in-plan decision" and "standalone project decision" create friction?
53	- Where do ratified decisions surface in Oculus — dedicated page, subsection of quest view, or global decisions log?
54	- Migration: leave existing prose decisions alone, or promote important ones retroactively into records?
55

59	- Astrolabe `Decision` / `DecisionAnalysis` types — `astrolabe/types.ts`
60	- Spider `InputRequestDoc` + `patron-input` block type
61	- Astrolabe decision-review engine
62	- Related: T3 Astrolabe root (`w-mnsxcp2m-816fb7b5b156`) — proposed owner for decisions book
63	- Related: T1.3 concurrent writes (`w-mnswwgah-7dca55bc359e`) — Astrolabe becoming a quest writer is the likely trigger there too

65	## Notes

# Decisions & ratification (v2 of the writ-substrate design)

_Imported from `.scratch/conversation-topics-as-writs.md` § "v2 sketch: decisions and ratification" (2026-04-10)._

## Goal

Make decisions — the conclusions reached inside quests and other inquiries — first-class, queryable records with attribution, timestamps, links, and a ratification flow. Today decisions live as prose inside quest bodies and are only discoverable by grep. The target: a decisions book owned by Astrolabe holding standalone `ProjectDecisionDoc` records that reference writs by id, with ratification routed through Spider's existing `InputRequestDoc` + `patron-input` machinery.

## Status

Parked. Deferred from v1 of the writ-substrate work as explicitly additive. The absence is analytical, not operational — no one has hit "I can't find what we decided about X" yet.

## Next Steps

Hold until the signal hits. The signal is Sean or Coco trying to answer "what did we decide about X?" and finding prose-grep insufficient. When it does, start with InputRequest schema relaxation (Phase 1 below) since that's the smallest independent slice and unlocks standalone ratification requests regardless of whether the full decisions book gets built.

## Context

**Schema sketch** (extends Astrolabe's existing `Decision` type):

```typescript
interface ProjectDecisionDoc extends Decision {
  // Inherited: id, scope, question, context, options, recommendation,
  //            rationale, selected, patronOverride, analysis
  status: 'draft' | 'proposed' | 'ratified' | 'superseded' | 'withdrawn';
  made_at: string;
  related_writs: string[];
  tags?: string[];
  supersedes?: string;
  patron?: string;
  coco_session?: string;
  drafted_by?: string;
  ratified_via?: string;  // InputRequest id
}
```

**Ratification reuses Spider's InputRequest machinery.** Coco drafts a decision, creates an `InputRequestDoc` asking "Ratify this decision?" with options `{ratify, amend, reject}` and `allowCustom: true`. On answer: `ratify` → status `ratified` with `selected` from recommendation; `amend` → `patronOverride` filled, status `ratified`; `reject` → status `withdrawn`.

**Schema relaxation needed.** `InputRequestDoc.rigId` and `engineId` are currently required. Standalone decisions have no rig — those fields need to become optional, or gain a discriminator for "standalone" vs "engine-initiated" requests.

**Decision → quest linking is many-to-many for free** via `decision.related_writs`. One decision can touch multiple quests; one quest can accumulate multiple decisions.

**Phased build (if/when taken on):**

1. InputRequest schema relaxation (standalone requests without `rigId`/`engineId`).
2. `ProjectDecisionDoc` type + decisions book.
3. Astrolabe ownership wiring.
4. Oculus surface for ratified decisions.

**Open:**

- Does extending the existing in-plan `Decision` type actually fit, or does sharing schema between "in-plan decision" and "standalone project decision" create friction?
- Where do ratified decisions surface in Oculus — dedicated page, subsection of quest view, or global decisions log?
- Migration: leave existing prose decisions alone, or promote important ones retroactively into records?

## References

- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
- Astrolabe `Decision` / `DecisionAnalysis` types — `astrolabe/types.ts`
- Spider `InputRequestDoc` + `patron-input` block type
- Astrolabe decision-review engine
- Related: T3 Astrolabe root (`w-mnsxcp2m-816fb7b5b156`) — proposed owner for decisions book
- Related: T1.3 concurrent writes (`w-mnswwgah-7dca55bc359e`) — Astrolabe becoming a quest writer is the likely trigger there too

## Notes

- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.

1	# Rename 'quest' — the word doesn't sit right
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Pick a better word than "quest" for the writ type that captures lightweight, working-note inquiry. The current name carries heroic-journey connotations that overshoot the mundane reality of most entries. Candidates on the table: `sketch`, `jotting`, `scribble` (or a better fourth option).
6	
7	## Status
8	
9	Active — exploring candidates. No decision yet.
10	
11	## Next Steps
12	
13	Check whether `sketch`, `jotting`, or `scribble` already appear in a different sense in the framework source or the guild metaphor docs (would disqualify the colliding candidates). Then pick one and do the rename pass: writ type name, skill directory, Coco agent instructions, metaphor doc, `nsg` CLI help text. Rename cost is bounded — fresh mechanism, no external dependencies.
14	
15	## Context
16	
17	"Quest" was the working name when the writ-substrate design shipped, and it's been rubbing wrong since. What we have is closer to a **parked thought** or a **working note**: a place to put a line of inquiry down so it doesn't get lost between sessions. Most entries are mundane. Calling them "quests" makes every stray thought sound like it's going on an odyssey.
18	
19	**Criteria for a good name:**
20	
21	- Matches the lightweight, "working note" feel of most entries
22	- Doesn't overpromise on structure or seriousness
23	- Fits the guild vocabulary (horological / craftwork / scholarly register)
24	- Works as a verb: "let me sketch this out", "jot that down"
25	- Survives pluralization and composition: `sketches` directory, `nsg sketch list`, "the decisions sketch"
26	
27	**Candidates:**
28	
29	- **Sketch** — draft-quality capture. Evokes the artist's notebook: quick lines, unfinished, meant to be worked up later. Pairs with "finished" artifacts elsewhere. Coco's current lean: closest to the "draft-quality, meant to be developed" semantics; works cleanly as noun and verb; pairs with the guild's craft register.
30	- **Jotting** — brief, informal written note. Punchier, more casual, accurate for the 80% of entries that never grow beyond a paragraph. Slightly awkward as a verb in instructions ("jotting-list" feels off).
31	- **Scribble** — most casual and honest about provisionality. Risks self-deprecating the mechanism into irrelevance; might read as dismissive when an autonomous planning agent consumes them.
32	- **Other ("workbook / atelier" register):** leaf, marginalia, notation, musing, inkling…
33	
34	## References
35	
36	- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
37	- Rename surface area: `.claude/skills/quests/`, `.claude/agents/coco.md`, `packages/framework/cli/` (CLI help), `/workspace/nexus/docs/guild-metaphor.md`
38	
39	## Notes
40	
41	- Worth checking: does the framework or metaphor doc already use any candidate word in a different sense?
42	- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.
43

# Rename 'quest' — the word doesn't sit right

## Goal

Pick a better word than "quest" for the writ type that captures lightweight, working-note inquiry. The current name carries heroic-journey connotations that overshoot the mundane reality of most entries. Candidates on the table: `sketch`, `jotting`, `scribble` (or a better fourth option).

## Status

Active — exploring candidates. No decision yet.

## Next Steps

Check whether `sketch`, `jotting`, or `scribble` already appear in a different sense in the framework source or the guild metaphor docs (would disqualify the colliding candidates). Then pick one and do the rename pass: writ type name, skill directory, Coco agent instructions, metaphor doc, `nsg` CLI help text. Rename cost is bounded — fresh mechanism, no external dependencies.

## Context

"Quest" was the working name when the writ-substrate design shipped, and it's been rubbing wrong since. What we have is closer to a **parked thought** or a **working note**: a place to put a line of inquiry down so it doesn't get lost between sessions. Most entries are mundane. Calling them "quests" makes every stray thought sound like it's going on an odyssey.

**Criteria for a good name:**

- Matches the lightweight, "working note" feel of most entries
- Doesn't overpromise on structure or seriousness
- Fits the guild vocabulary (horological / craftwork / scholarly register)
- Works as a verb: "let me sketch this out", "jot that down"
- Survives pluralization and composition: `sketches` directory, `nsg sketch list`, "the decisions sketch"

**Candidates:**

- **Sketch** — draft-quality capture. Evokes the artist's notebook: quick lines, unfinished, meant to be worked up later. Pairs with "finished" artifacts elsewhere. Coco's current lean: closest to the "draft-quality, meant to be developed" semantics; works cleanly as noun and verb; pairs with the guild's craft register.
- **Jotting** — brief, informal written note. Punchier, more casual, accurate for the 80% of entries that never grow beyond a paragraph. Slightly awkward as a verb in instructions ("jotting-list" feels off).
- **Scribble** — most casual and honest about provisionality. Risks self-deprecating the mechanism into irrelevance; might read as dismissive when an autonomous planning agent consumes them.
- **Other ("workbook / atelier" register):** leaf, marginalia, notation, musing, inkling…

## References

- Parent quest: `w-mnswvmj7-2112b86f710a`
- Rename surface area: `.claude/skills/quests/`, `.claude/agents/coco.md`, `packages/framework/cli/` (CLI help), `/workspace/nexus/docs/guild-metaphor.md`

## Notes

- Worth checking: does the framework or metaphor doc already use any candidate word in a different sense?
- 2026-04-10: opened as child of writ substrate quest.
